hq: 2261 Market Street STE 85861, San Francisco, CA 94114

system_description.overview: Parsed is an AI lab that provides a post-training service and platform helping organizations own and continually improve domain-specific frontier LLMs. The platform enables customers to evaluate, fine-tune, and run dedicated inference on models built specifically for them across multi-cloud environments, serving healthcare, legal, insurance, finance, and other industries.

products.Evaluation & Training Platform: Post-training workflows including evaluation harness builder and editor, supervised fine-tuning linked to evaluation harness and customer data, reinforcement learning (RLHF, DPO, GRPO, etc.) and continual learning loops for domain-specific LLMs.
products.Inference Infrastructure: Dedicated, region-specific inference clusters deployed across multi-cloud environments with enterprise-grade SLAs and monitoring.
products.Onboarding & Integration Services: White-glove onboarding and setup, including integration of Parsed's evaluation, training, and inference pipelines into customer environments.
products.Ongoing Engineering & Support: Continuous support from Parsed's research and engineering teams, covering performance monitoring, retraining workflows, infrastructure scaling, and compliance safeguards.

infrastructure.cloud_provider: Supabase (carve-out subservice organization for data center services)
regions: Not explicitly disclosed; multi-cloud environments mentioned
infrastructure.physical_presence: No physical offices in scope; all data processed on cloud services
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (hosted in high availability data centers with multiple availability zones per Supabase)
additional_cloud: Render (VPC for app.parsed.com, Job Runner, LLM Manager)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: System hosted in Supabase VPC with segmented VPCs and access control lists (ACLs). Network diagram shows separate Supabase VPC (Data API, Auth API, DB, Storage API) and Render VPC (app.parsed.com, Job Runner, LLM Manager) connected via public internet, with Baseten hosting deployed LLMs.
network_architecture.api_gateway: Internet Gateway shown on both Supabase VPC and Render VPC; firewalls configured on application gateway
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on the application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management.
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS employed via Supabase to identify and protect against threats in conjunction with security policy network settings. Firewalls and intrusion detection tools used to prevent unauthorized access and detect external security threats (CC6.6.1).
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access done through a bastion host with a virtual private network (VPN)
network_architecture.tls: User requests encrypted using Transport Layer Security (TLS) with certificates from established third-party certificate authority
network_architecture.content_filtering: Content filtering system routed through proxy server; all systems restricted to internet through this system
network_architecture.topology: Users -> Public Internet -> Supabase VPC (Data API, Auth API, DB, Storage API) + Render VPC (app.parsed.com, Job Runner, LLM Manager) -> Baseten (Deployed LLMs). LLM Manager in Render manages and calls LLMs on Baseten. Job Runner processes jobs and LLM deployments between Supabase and Render.

application_architecture.pattern: Hybrid (multi-cloud microservices-style with separate VPCs for data layer, application layer, and inference layer)
compute: Render (web services, Job Runner, LLM Manager)
application_architecture.api_style: REST (inferred from Data API, Auth API, Storage API shown in network diagram)
application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test and production environments (CC8.1.4: changes developed and tested in separate environment before implementation)

data_storage.backup_frequency: Suitable backups taken and maintained per Backup Policy; specific frequency not disclosed

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: Supabase Auth API (shown in network diagram); passwords controlled through Supabase password policy
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1); MFA on source code management tool (CC8.1.3); two-factor authentication required for employee email access
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination of employment
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Controlled through Supabase password policy; includes periodic forced changes, password expiry, complexity requirements; accounts disabled after limited unsuccessful logon attempts; guest and anonymous logins not allowed; unattended desktops locked within a time of inactivity

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used to encrypt data at rest (CC6.1.5)
encryption.key_management: Cloud provider key management service; access to production environment access keys restricted to authorized individuals (CC6.1.5)

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Source code management tool with MFA access (specific tool not named; likely GitHub given MFA + branch rules)
ci_cd_devops.ci_cd_tool: Not explicitly named; formal change management policy in place with internal tool tracking from initiation through deployment and validation
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to ensure every merge request to move change to production environment requires additional approval (CC8.1.2)
ci_cd_devops.iac: Baseline configurations retained within infrastructure as code; configuration management tool used for implementation and rollback capability (CC7.1.1)
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management with monthly patch management reviews by CTO; workstations configured for automatic updates and scanned for patch compliance daily (CC7.5.1)

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Monitoring tools used for continuous system capacity and performance monitoring (specific tool not named; CC5.2.1)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled to monitor administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions at application and infrastructure level, changes to functions, security configurations, permissions, and roles (CC7.2.1)
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events; predefined thresholds trigger alerts (CC5.2.1, CC7.2.1)

third_party_services.purpose: Primary subservice organization for data center services, database (PostgreSQL), authentication, storage APIs, and VPC hosting
Cloud platform hosting app.parsed.com web application, Job Runner, and LLM Manager services in a separate VPC
Inference hosting platform for deployed LLMs; LLM Manager in Render calls and manages models on Baseten

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed and configured on all production servers with continuous automatic scanning; definitions updated weekly for production servers; also installed on Mac workstations and laptops (CC6.8.1)
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications with frequency adjusted as required; periodic internal scans per audit calendar (CC7.1.2)
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party; high/critical findings remediated in timely manner (CC4.1.1)

excluded_criteria: CC6.4 - Physical access to facilities: Supabase responsible for physical security of data center; not in scope
A1.2 - Environmental protections: Parsed does not have an office; environmental controls managed by building management / Supabase
Processing Integrity - Determined not relevant to the system
Privacy - Determined not relevant to the system
notable_controls: CC8.1.2: Branch protection rules enforce additional approval for all merge requests to production
CC8.1.3: Source code changes logged, time-stamped, attributed to author; access restricted via MFA
CC7.1.1: Infrastructure as code with configuration management tool and rollback capability
CC6.1.3: Per-tenant data segregation - system components configured so company and customer access is segmented from other customer accounts
CC7.4.1: BCDR plan tested annually per defined testing schedule for different loss scenarios
CC6.6.1: Firewalls and intrusion detection tools configured to prevent unauthorized access and detect external threats
CC9.2.1: Vendor management policy with annual SOC report reviews and quarterly risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports

infrastructure.rationale: Multi-cloud architecture across Supabase, Render, and Baseten with multi-AZ failover is a positive. However, reliance on Supabase (a managed platform, not a hyperscaler like AWS/GCP) as the primary data center subservice is unconventional. No CDN mentioned. No specific regions disclosed. IaC mentioned but tools not named. The multi-cloud spread across Render and Baseten shows intentional architecture, but overall infrastructure maturity is moderate for an AI inference company.

application_architecture.rationale: Hybrid multi-service architecture with clear separation of concerns (data layer in Supabase VPC, application in Render VPC, inference on Baseten). Branch protection and code review enforced. Separate dev/test/prod environments. However, no programming languages, frameworks, or testing practices disclosed. No API style explicitly stated. Architecture appears reasonably modular but specifics are thin.

data_layer.rationale: Supabase PostgreSQL for database with storage APIs. Data classification policy, retention policy, and per-tenant segregation all present. Multi-AZ via Supabase. However, no caching layer mentioned, no data warehouse, backup frequency not specified, and only a single database technology disclosed. For an AI company handling large model training data, the data layer disclosure is surprisingly minimal.

security_posture.rationale: Good layered security: IDS/IPS via Supabase, firewalls on application gateway, MFA for admin access and source code, endpoint protection with antivirus on all servers and workstations, monthly vulnerability scanning, annual external pen testing, encryption at rest and in transit, VPN for remote access, content filtering. Weaknesses: no WAF mentioned, no SIEM named, no specific encryption standards (TLS version, AES-256, etc.), cybersecurity insurance status unknown.

devops_maturity.rationale: IaC with configuration management and rollback capability mentioned. Branch protection with required approval for production merges. Source code management with MFA. Formal change management policy with emergency change process. Separate environments. However, no CI/CD tool named, no container orchestration mentioned, no specific IaC tool named. The practices are described generically without naming specific tooling.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually. Annual testing of BCDR plans per defined schedule for different loss scenarios. Multi-AZ failover via Supabase with spinning up multiple servers across availability zones. DR plan identifies critical IT applications, operating systems, personnel, data files, and time frames based on business impact analysis. However, no RTO/RPO disclosed, no multi-region failover, and backup frequency not specified.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Three cloud vendors identified (Supabase, Render, Baseten), which provides some diversity. However, all three are relatively small/niche platforms rather than hyperscalers, introducing risk if any of them has service disruptions or goes out of business. Heavy concentration on Supabase for all data, auth, and storage. Vendor management policy in place with annual SOC reviews and quarterly assessments.

overall.rationale: Parsed demonstrates a reasonable security posture for an early-stage AI company achieving its first SOC 2 Type 1. The multi-cloud architecture shows intentional design. Core security controls (MFA, encryption, IDS/IPS, pen testing, vulnerability scanning, BCDR) are all present. However, the report is notably thin on technology specifics -- no programming languages, no named CI/CD tools, no named monitoring tools, no encryption standards, and significant reliance on smaller cloud platforms rather than hyperscalers. The Type 1 (point-in-time) nature means operational effectiveness has not been tested over a period.

red_flags: Type 1 report only (point-in-time assessment, no observation period) -- operational effectiveness of controls has not been tested
Only Security trust criteria covered; Processing Integrity and Privacy excluded as 'not relevant' despite handling customer training data and LLM models
Heavy reliance on smaller/niche cloud platforms (Supabase, Render, Baseten) rather than hyperscalers, creating vendor viability risk
No WAF (Web Application Firewall) mentioned despite operating web-facing services
No RTO/RPO disclosed despite having BCDR plan
Public internet used for communication between Supabase VPC and Render VPC (shown in network diagram), creating potential attack surface
yellow_flags: No specific programming languages, frameworks, or testing tools disclosed
No named CI/CD tool, monitoring/APM tool, or SIEM platform
TLS version not specified; encryption at rest method not specified (no AES-256 or specific standard cited)
Backup frequency not explicitly defined in the report
SSO not mentioned for customer-facing or internal access
Very small team (org chart shows ~8 people) which creates key-person risk
Supabase carries the weight of data, auth, and storage -- single point of failure for the data layer
No Availability or Confidentiality trust criteria included in scope
green_flags: Clean unqualified opinion with zero exceptions
Multi-cloud architecture with intentional separation of concerns across three providers
MFA enforced for admin production access, source code management, and employee email
Branch protection with required approval for production deployments
Infrastructure as code with configuration management and rollback capability
Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party
Monthly automated vulnerability scanning with remediation tracking
Annual BCDR testing with defined loss scenarios
Vendor management policy with SOC report reviews and quarterly risk assessments
Formal incident response management process with root cause analysis
Access revocation within one business day of termination
Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts
key_observations: Network diagram reveals Baseten as a third critical cloud provider not listed as a formal subservice organization -- only Supabase is carved out as a subservice org, yet Baseten hosts the deployed LLMs which is arguably the most critical component of Parsed's service
Render is also not listed as a subservice organization despite hosting the primary application (app.parsed.com), Job Runner, and LLM Manager -- this is a significant scope gap

The report uses Supabase as both the database platform and the subservice organization for 'data center services', which is unusual since Supabase itself runs on AWS -- this creates a transitive dependency that is not addressed
For an AI company building custom LLMs, the report is surprisingly silent on GPU infrastructure, model storage, training compute, and data pipeline tooling

The org chart shows a very lean team (~8 people) with CEO, Chief Scientist, CTO, 2 Research Scientists, 1 Product Engineer, and 1 Strategy & Operations person -- this is extremely small for the enterprise services described

The company claims 'multi-cloud environments' and 'region-specific inference' in its service description but the report provides no details on which regions or clouds are used for inference beyond the Baseten box in the network diagram
Privacy trust criteria excluded as 'not relevant' is questionable given the company handles customer data for LLM fine-tuning
